Article.ArticleTitle: Impact of mask wearing time on fluid consumption and physical activity during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Abstract.AbstractText: To examine the associations between mask-wearing on fluid consumption and physical activity behaviors during the COVID-19 pandemic.

137 college students (female, 72.5%; age, 26 ± 9 y) completed a survey detailing their fluid intake, physical activity behaviors, and time spent wearing a mask throughout the day during the previous month in the Fall 2020 academic semester.

Increased daily mask wearing time was not associated with total fluid intake (p > 0.05). Participants had greater odds of being 'somewhat active' compared to 'inactive' with an increase in mask wearing time (OR = 1.23 [1.03, 1.47], p = 0.022).

Wearing a mask during the COVID-19 pandemic did not influence fluid intake behaviors, however, it increased the likelihood of reported greater levels of physical activity. These factors may be related to an individual being more likely to globally adopt healthier behaviors, however, this needs further exploration.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: Copyright © 2025 Zaplatosch, Butts, Scarneo-Miller and Adams.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: WMA receives royalties from Springer Nature and sits on the following advisory boards; Medical and Science Advisory Board for the Korey Stringer Institute, Sport Advisory Council for the Wu Tsai Human Performance Alliance, Expert Advisory Board for Emerja Corporation, Expert Advisory Board for My Normative, and is the owner of Adams Sports Medicine Consulting LLC. The remaining authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.
